Targeting A20 (tumor necrosis factor alpha-induced protein 3) decreases glioma stem cell (GSC) survival and tumor growth. Glioblastomas are deadly cancers that display a functional cellular hierarchy maintained by self-renewing glioblastoma stem cells (GSCs; rare, highly proliferative cells within the tumor that can give rise to new tumors). GSCs are regulated by molecular pathways distinct from the bulk tumor (the main mass of cancer cells) that may be useful therapeutic targets. We determined that A20 (tumor necrosis factor alpha-induced protein 3, a regulator of cell survival and the NF-kappaB pathway) is overexpressed in GSCs relative to non-stem glioblastoma cells at both the mRNA (messenger RNA) and protein levels. To determine the functional significance of A20 in GSCs, we targeted A20 expression with lentiviral-mediated delivery of short hairpin RNA (shRNA; small RNA molecules used to silence specific genes). Inhibiting A20 expression decreased GSC growth and survival through mechanisms associated with decreased cell-cycle progression (the series of events that take place in a cell leading to its division into two daughter cells) and decreased phosphorylation of p65/RelA (a component of the NF-kappaB transcription factor complex involved in regulating gene expression). Elevated levels of A20 in GSCs contributed to apoptotic resistance: GSCs were less susceptible to TNFalpha (tumor necrosis factor alpha)-induced cell death than matched non-stem glioma cells, but A20 knockdown sensitized GSCs to TNFalpha-mediated apoptosis. The decreased survival of GSCs upon A20 knockdown contributed to the reduced ability of these cells to self-renew in primary and secondary neurosphere formation assays (in vitro cultures used to study neural stem cells). The tumorigenic potential of GSCs was decreased with A20 targeting, resulting in increased survival of mice bearing human glioma xenografts (transplanted human glioma tissue grown in immunocompromised mice). Tracking the fate of glomerular epithelial cells in vivo using serial multiphoton imaging (MPM, a technique that captures high-resolution images of living tissues over time) in novel mouse models with fluorescent lineage tags (FLTs, genetic modifications that allow visualization of cell lineages). Podocytes are critical in the maintenance of a healthy glomerular filter (glomerulus, a cluster of capillaries in the kidney); however, they have been difficult to study in the intact kidney because of technical limitations. Here we report the development of serial multiphoton microscopy (MPM) of the same glomeruli over several days to visualize the motility of podocytes and parietal epithelial cells (PECs) (cells lining the Bowman's capsule) in vivo. In podocin-GFP mice, podocytes formed sporadic multicellular clusters after unilateral ureteral ligation (a surgical procedure that obstructs one ureter) and migrated into the parietal Bowman's capsule. The tracking of single cells in podocin-confetti mice featuring cell-specific expression of CFP, GFP, YFP or RFP (fluorescent proteins that emit light at different wavelengths) revealed the simultaneous migration of multiple podocytes. In phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (PEPCK, an enzyme involved in gluconeogenesis) -GFP mice, serial MPM found PEC-to-podocyte migration and nanotubule connections (tiny tubes that facilitate communication between cells). Our data support a highly dynamic rather than a static nature of the glomerular environment and cellular composition. Future application of this new approach should advance our understanding of the mechanisms of glomerular injury and regeneration.

The stimulatory potency of T cell antigens is influenced by the formation of the immunological synapse (a specialized interface between T cells and antigen-presenting cells). T cell activation is predicated on the interaction between the T cell receptor (TCR; a protein complex on the surface of T cells that recognizes specific peptide antigens) and peptide-major histocompatibility (pMHC; a complex of a peptide and major histocompatibility complex molecules that presents peptides to T cells) ligands. The factors that determine the stimulatory potency of a pMHC molecule remain unclear. We describe results showing that a peptide exhibiting many hallmarks of a weak agonist (a substance that activates a biological process) stimulates T cells to proliferate more than the wild-type agonist ligand. An in silico approach (computer-based modeling) suggested that the inability to form the central supramolecular activation cluster (cSMAC; a dense aggregation of signaling proteins at the immunological synapse) could underlie the increased proliferation. This conclusion was supported by experiments that showed that enhancing cSMAC formation reduced the stimulatory capacity of the weak peptide. Our studies highlight the fact that a complex interplay of factors (multiple variables and mechanisms) determines the quality of a T cell antigen.

Memory and Modularity in Cell-Fate Decision Making Genetically identical cells sharing an environment can display markedly different phenotypes (outcomes observable at the cellular level). It is often unclear how much of this variation derives from chance, external signals, or attempts by individual cells to exert autonomous phenotypic programs (cellular behaviors). By observing thousands of cells for hundreds of consecutive generations under constant conditions, we dissect the stochastic decision between a solitary, motile state (cells moving freely) and a chained, sessile state (cells forming stationary chains) in Bacillus subtilis (a soil bacterium known for its ability to form biofilms). We show that the motile state is'memoryless' (cells do not remember past states), exhibiting no autonomous control over the time spent in the state. In contrast, the time spent as connected chains of cells is tightly controlled, enforcing coordination among related cells in the multicellular state (cells working together). We show that the three-protein regulatory circuit governing the decision is modular (composed of distinct, interchangeable parts), as initiation and maintenance of chaining are genetically separable functions (can be independently regulated). As stimulation of the same initiating pathway triggers biofilm formation (a cluster of cells adhering to surfaces), we argue that autonomous timing allows a trial commitment to multicellularity (an initial step towards forming a multicellular structure) that external signals could extend.

791050: The relation between past exposure to fine particulate air pollution (particulate matter <2.5 μm in diameter) and prevalent high symptoms of anxiety: observational cohort study OBJECTIVE To determine whether higher past exposure to particulate air pollution (particulate matter <2.5 μm in diameter) is associated with prevalent high symptoms of anxiety. DESIGN Observational cohort study. SETTING Nurses' Health Study. PARTICIPANTS 71,271 women enrolled in the Nurses' Health Study residing throughout the contiguous United States who had valid estimates on exposure to particulate matter (PM) for at least one exposure period of interest and data on anxiety symptoms. MAIN OUTCOME MEASURES Meaningfully high symptoms of anxiety, defined as a score of 6 points or greater on the phobic anxiety subscale of the Crown-Crisp index, administered in 2004. RESULTS The 71,271 eligible women were aged between 57 and 85 years (mean 70 years) at the time of assessment of anxiety symptoms, with a prevalence of high anxiety symptoms of 15%. Exposure to particulate matter was characterized using estimated average exposure to particulate matter <2.5 μm in diameter (PM2.5) and 2.5 to 10 μm in diameter (PM2.5-10) in the one month, three months, six months, one year, and 15 years prior to assessment of anxiety symptoms, and residential distance to the nearest major road two years prior to assessment. Significantly increased odds of high anxiety symptoms were observed with higher exposure to PM2.5 for multiple averaging periods (for example, odds ratio per 10 µg/m(3) increase in prior one month average PM2.5 (fine particulate matter)). Cold Exposure Promotes Atherosclerotic Plaque Growth and Instability via UCP1-Dependent Lipolysis (process of breaking down fat into fatty acids and glycerol). Molecular mechanisms underlying the cold-associated high cardiovascular risk (increased risk of heart disease and stroke) remain unknown. Here, we show that the cold-triggered food-intake-independent lipolysis (lipolysis occurring without changes in food intake) significantly increased plasma levels of small low-density lipoprotein (LDL) remnants (small, dense LDL particles), leading to accelerated development of atherosclerotic lesions (buildup of fatty deposits in arteries) in mice. In two genetic mouse knockout models (apolipoprotein E(-/-) [ApoE(-/-)] and LDL receptor(-/-) [Ldlr(-/-)] mice), persistent cold exposure stimulated atherosclerotic plaque growth by increasing lipid deposition (accumulation of fats). Furthermore, marked increase of inflammatory cells (white blood cells involved in immune response) and plaque-associated microvessels (small blood vessels within plaques) were detected in the cold-acclimated ApoE(-/-) and Ldlr(-/-) mice, leading to plaque instability (risk of plaque rupture and subsequent thrombosis). Deletion of uncoupling protein 1 (UCP1), a key mitochondrial protein involved in thermogenesis (production of heat) in brown adipose tissue (BAT, specialized fat tissue), in the ApoE(-/-) strain completely protected mice from the cold-induced atherosclerotic lesions. Cold acclimation markedly reduced plasma levels of adiponectin (protein hormone that regulates glucose levels and fatty acid breakdown), and systemic delivery of adiponectin protected ApoE(-/-) mice from plaque development. These findings provide mechanistic insights on low-temperature-associated cardiovascular risks (risks related to heart and blood vessel diseases associated with exposure to cold temperatures).

Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs, structures formed by neutrophils to trap pathogens) enriched in oxidized mitochondrial DNA are interferogenic (promoting interferon production) and contribute to lupus-like disease. Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) are implicated in autoimmunity, but how they are generated and their roles in sterile inflammation remain unclear. Ribonucleoprotein immune complexes (RNP ICs, aggregates of RNA and proteins that can trigger immune responses) induce NETosis (NET formation), and require mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (ROS, molecules produced during cellular metabolism) for maximal NET stimulation. After RNP IC stimulation of neutrophils, mitochondria become hypopolarized (loss of membrane potential) and translocate to the cell surface. Extracellular release of oxidized mitochondrial DNA is proinflammatory (causing inflammation) in vitro, and when this DNA is injected into mice, it stimulates type I interferon (IFN, signaling molecule involved in innate immunity) signaling through a pathway dependent on the DNA sensor STING (stimulator of interferon genes). Mitochondrial ROS are also necessary for spontaneous NETosis (NET formation without external stimuli) of low-density granulocytes from individuals with systemic lupus erythematosus (chronic autoimmune disorder characterized by inflammation and tissue damage). This was also observed in individuals with chronic granulomatous disease (genetic disorder leading to impaired phagocyte function), who lack NADPH oxidase activity (enzyme involved in ROS production) but still develop autoimmunity and type I IFN signatures. Mitochondrial ROS inhibition in vivo reduces disease severity and type I IFN responses in a mouse model of lupus. Together, these findings highlight a role for mitochondria in the generation not only of NETs but also of pro-inflammatory oxidized mitochondrial DNA in autoimmune diseases.

Restoration of chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA, a selective mechanism for degradation of cytosolic proteins in lysosomes, contributing to the removal of altered proteins as part of the cellular quality-control systems) in aging liver improves cellular maintenance and hepatic function. We have previously found that CMA activity declines in aged organisms (organisms with advanced age) and have proposed that this failure in cellular clearance (removal of damaged or misfolded proteins) could contribute to the accumulation of altered proteins, the abnormal cellular homeostasis (state of equilibrium within the cell), and, eventually, the functional loss characteristic of aged organisms. To determine whether these negative features of aging can be prevented by maintaining efficient autophagic activity until late in life, in this work we have corrected the CMA defect in aged rodents. We have generated a double transgenic mouse model in which the amount of the lysosomal receptor for CMA, previously shown to decrease in abundance with age (reduction in the number of receptors available to bind and degrade cytosolic proteins), can be modulated. We have analyzed in this model the consequences of preventing the age-dependent decrease in receptor abundance in aged rodents at the cellular and organ levels. We show here that CMA activity is maintained until advanced ages if the decrease in the receptor abundance is prevented and that preservation of autophagic activity (process by which cells break down and recycle their own components) is associated with lower intracellular accumulation of damaged proteins, better ability to handle protein damage, and improved organ function.

Endothelium-mediated relaxation of porcine collateral-dependent arterioles is improved by exercise training. BACKGROUND Endothelium-dependent modulation of coronary tone is impaired in the collateral-dependent coronary microcirculation (small blood vessels supplying blood to an area of the heart when the main supply is blocked). We used a porcine model of chronic coronary occlusion and collateral development to evaluate the hypothesis that exercise training enhances endothelium-mediated relaxation and increases endothelial nitric oxide synthase (ecNOS, an enzyme that produces nitric oxide, a molecule that relaxes blood vessels) mRNA levels of collateral-dependent microvasculature. METHODS AND RESULTS Adult female miniature swine were subjected to chronic, progressive ameroid occlusion of the proximal left circumflex coronary artery (LCx, a major coronary artery supplying blood to the left ventricle of the heart); after 2 months, animals were randomly exposed to 16-week exercise-training (EX group; treadmill running) or sedentary (SED group; cage confinement) protocols. After completion of EX or SED programs, coronary arterioles (approximately 100 microm in diameter, small blood vessels in the heart) were isolated from collateral-dependent LCx (distal to occlusion) and nonoccluded left anterior descending coronary artery (LAD) regions of each heart. Arterioles were studied by in vitro videomicroscopy (a technique to visualize blood vessels under a microscope) or frozen for ecNOS mRNA analysis (RT-PCR techniques, methods to measure the amount of specific genes). 1148122: Regulatory and metabolic rewiring during laboratory evolution of ethanol tolerance in E. coli (Escherichia coli, a common gut bacterium) Understanding the genetic basis of adaptation is a central problem in biology. However, revealing the underlying molecular mechanisms has been challenging as changes in fitness may result from perturbations to many pathways, any of which may contribute relatively little. We have developed a combined experimental/computational framework to address this problem and used it to understand the genetic basis of ethanol tolerance in Escherichia coli. We used fitness profiling (a method to measure how well an organism grows under specific conditions) to measure the consequences of single-locus perturbations in the context of ethanol exposure. A module-level computational analysis (an approach that considers groups of genes or proteins together rather than individually) was then used to reveal the organization of the contributing loci into cellular processes and regulatory pathways (e.g. osmoregulation (the regulation of water balance within cells) and cell-wall biogenesis (the process of building the cell wall)) whose modifications significantly affect ethanol tolerance. Strikingly, we discovered that a dominant component of adaptation involves metabolic rewiring (changes in metabolic pathways) that boosts intracellular ethanol degradation and assimilation (the conversion of ethanol into useful cellular components). Through phenotypic and metabolomic analysis (assessing observable characteristics and metabolic profiles) of laboratory-evolved ethanol-tolerant strains, we investigated naturally accessible pathways of ethanol tolerance. Remarkably, these laboratory-evolved strains, by and large, follow the same adaptive paths as inferred from our coarse-grained search of the fitness landscape (a simplified representation of the range of possible genotypes and their relative fitness).
1180972: Genetics of obesity in adult adoptees and their biological siblings. An adoption study of genetic effects on obesity in adulthood was carried out in which adoptees separated from their natural parents very early in life were compared with their biological full and half siblings reared by their natural parents (individuals raised by non-biological parents). The adoptees represented four groups who by sampling from a larger population were categorised as either thin, medium weight, overweight, or obese. Weight and height were obtained for 115 full siblings of 57 adoptees and for 850 half siblings of 341 adoptees. In full siblings, body mass index (BMI, kg/m2) significantly increased with weight of the adoptees (statistically significant association). Body mass index of the half siblings showed a steady but weaker increase across the four weight groups of adoptees (less pronounced but still present association). There were no significant interactions with sex of the adoptees, sex of the siblings, or (for the half siblings) sex of the common parent. In contrast with the findings in half siblings and (previously) the natural parents, there was a striking, significant increase in body mass index between full siblings of overweight and obese adoptees (stronger genetic influence observed). The degree of fatness in adults living in the same environment appears to be influenced by genetic factors independent of sex, which may include polygenic as well as major gene effects on obesity (genetic predisposition contributing to obesity regardless of gender, potentially involving multiple genes or a single major gene).

“Rapid-Impact Interventions”: How a Policy of Integrated Control for Africa's Neglected Tropical Diseases Could Benefit the Poor Over the past two decades there have been significant achievements in the control of a handful of important human tropical infections (infections prevalent in tropical regions) [1]. These achievements include the substantive reductions in the prevalence and incidence of the so-called neglected diseases such as lymphatic filariasis (a parasitic infection causing chronic swelling), onchocerciasis (caused by the parasitic worm Onchocerca volvulus leading to skin and eye disease), guinea worm (caused by the parasitic worm Dracunculus medinensis leading to painful blisters), leprosy (caused by Mycobacterium leprae, a bacterial infection), and trachoma (caused by Chlamydia trachomatis, a bacterial infection leading to blindness) (Box 1) [2]. Each of these neglected diseases is a poverty-promoting and often stigmatizing condition occurring primarily in rural areas of low-income countries (Box 2) [3]. They are ancient afflictions, described in the Bible and other ancient texts, which have burdened humanity for millennia [3]. But now, as a result of aggressive regional vertical interventions (targeted, specialized programs aimed at specific diseases), there is a possibility that some neglected tropical infections could be eventually controlled to the point of elimination in some areas of endemicity (regions where a disease is regularly found) [2–8]. In the case of guinea worm infection, disease eradication might also soon be possible [9]. Box 2. Aptamer-functionalized lipid nanoparticles targeting osteoblasts as a novel RNA interference (RNAi)-based bone anabolic strategy (process of increasing bone formation and density) Currently, major concerns about the safety and efficacy of RNAi-based bone anabolic strategies still exist because of the lack of direct osteoblast-specific delivery systems for osteogenic small interfering RNAs (siRNAs) (short double-stranded RNA molecules used to silence specific genes). Here we screened the aptamer CH6 by cell-SELEX (cellular systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment), specifically targeting both rat and human osteoblasts, and then we developed CH6 aptamer–functionalized lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) (nanoscale carriers composed of lipids) encapsulating osteogenic pleckstrin homology domain-containing family O member 1 (Plekho1) siRNA (CH6-LNPs-siRNA). Our results showed that CH6 facilitated in vitro osteoblast-selective uptake of Plekho1 siRNA, mainly via macropinocytosis (a non-specific endocytic process where cells take in large volumes of extracellular fluid and associated solutes), and boosted in vivo osteoblast-specific Plekho1 gene silencing, which promoted bone formation, improved bone microarchitecture, increased bone mass and enhanced mechanical properties in both osteopenic (bone loss) and healthy rodents. These results indicate that osteoblast-specific aptamer-functionalized LNPs could act as a new RNAi-based bone anabolic strategy, advancing the targeted delivery selectivity of osteogenic siRNAs from the tissue level to the cellular level.

1471041: High abundance of plasma cells secreting transglutaminase 2–specific IgA autoantibodies (antibodies targeting the enzyme transglutaminase 2) with limited somatic hypermutation (a process where antibodies adapt to better recognize antigens through genetic changes) in celiac disease intestinal lesions. Celiac disease is an immune-mediated disorder in which mucosal autoantibodies to the enzyme transglutaminase 2 (TG2) are generated in response to the exogenous antigen gluten in individuals who express human leukocyte antigen HLA-DQ2 or HLA-DQ8 (ref. 3). We assessed in a comprehensive and nonbiased manner the IgA anti-TG2 response by expression cloning of the antibody repertoire of ex vivo–isolated intestinal antibody-secreting cells (ASCs; cells that produce and secrete antibodies). We found that TG2-specific plasma cells are markedly expanded within the duodenal mucosa (lining of the small intestine) in individuals with active celiac disease. TG2-specific antibodies were of high affinity yet showed little adaptation by somatic mutations. Unlike infection-induced peripheral blood plasmablasts (short-lived, rapidly dividing plasma cells), the TG2-specific ASCs had not recently proliferated and were not short-lived ex vivo. Altogether, these observations demonstrate that there is a germline repertoire with high affinity for TG2 that may favor massive generation of autoreactive B cells (B cells that mistakenly target self-antigens). TG2-specific antibodies did not block enzymatic activity and served as substrates for TG2-mediated crosslinking when expressed as IgD or IgM but not as IgA1 or IgG1. This could result in preferential recruitment of plasma cells from naive IgD- and IgM-expressing B cells, thus possibly explaining why the antibody response to TG2 bears signs of a primary immune response (an initial immune reaction rather than a secondary one).

The Bile Acid Chenodeoxycholic Acid (CDCA, a primary bile acid involved in lipid digestion and absorption) Increases Human Brown Adipose Tissue (BAT, specialized fat tissue that burns energy to generate heat) Activity. The interest in brown adipose tissue as a target to combat metabolic disease has recently been renewed with the discovery of functional BAT in humans. In rodents, BAT can be activated by bile acids, which activate type 2 iodothyronine deiodinase (D2, an enzyme that converts inactive thyroid hormone into active form) in BAT via the G-coupled protein receptor TGR5 (a membrane protein that senses bile acids and activates intracellular signaling), resulting in increased oxygen consumption and energy expenditure. Here we examined the effects of oral supplementation of the bile acid chenodeoxycholic acid (CDCA) on human BAT activity. Treatment of 12 healthy female subjects with CDCA for 2 days resulted in increased BAT activity. Whole-body energy expenditure was also increased upon CDCA treatment. In vitro treatment of primary human brown adipocytes (cells from the brown fat tissue) derived with CDCA or specific TGR5 agonists (molecules that bind to TGR5 and mimic its natural ligands) increased mitochondrial uncoupling (a process that leads to heat production) and D2 expression, an effect that was absent in human primary white adipocytes (cells from the white fat tissue). These findings identify bile acids as a target to activate BAT in humans.

CONTEXT One key target of the United Nations Millennium Development goals (MDGs) is to reduce the prevalence of underweight among children younger than 5 years by half between 1990 and 2015. OBJECTIVE To estimate trends in childhood underweight by geographic regions of the world. Effect of physical activity on cognitive function in older adults at risk for Alzheimer disease: a randomized trial. Amyloid-DNA Composites of Bacterial Biofilms Stimulate Autoimmunity. Research on the human microbiome has established that commensal and pathogenic bacteria can influence obesity, cancer, and autoimmunity through mechanisms mostly unknown (processes underlying these influences remain largely unexplored). We found that a component of bacterial biofilms, the amyloid protein curli (a fibrous extracellular matrix protein), irreversibly formed fibers with bacterial DNA during biofilm formation. This interaction accelerated amyloid polymerization (formation of larger aggregates from smaller units) and created potent immunogenic complexes (structures capable of triggering an immune response) that activated immune cells, including dendritic cells (specialized antigen-presenting cells), to produce cytokines such as type I interferons (pro-inflammatory signaling molecules), which are pathogenic in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE; an autoimmune disorder characterized by chronic inflammation and tissue damage). When given systemically, curli-DNA composites triggered immune activation and production of autoantibodies (antibodies directed against self-antigens) in lupus-prone and wild-type mice. We also found that the infection of lupus-prone mice with curli-producing bacteria triggered higher autoantibody titers (levels of autoantibodies in the blood) compared to curli-deficient bacteria. These data provide a mechanism by which the microbiome and biofilm-producing enteric infections may contribute to the progression of SLE and point to a potential molecular target for treatment of autoimmunity.

A genetic screen identifies an LKB1–MARK signalling axis controlling the Hippo–YAP pathway (a key signalling cascade regulating stem cell activity and organ size). To identify components of this pathway, we performed an RNAi-based kinome screen (screening method using RNA interference to inhibit individual kinases) in human cells. Our screen identified several kinases not previously associated with Hippo signalling (signalling network regulating cell proliferation and organ size) that control multiple cellular processes. One of the hits, LKB1 (a serine/threonine protein kinase), is a common tumour suppressor (gene that normally prevents cancer by inhibiting cell division) whose mechanism of action is only partially understood. We demonstrate that LKB1 acts through its substrates of the microtubule affinity-regulating kinase (MARK) family (proteins that phosphorylate other proteins) to regulate the localization of the polarity determinant Scribble (protein involved in establishing cell polarity) and the activity of the core Hippo kinases (kinases that transmit signals from the cell membrane to the nucleus). Our data also indicate that YAP (Yes-associated protein, a transcriptional co-activator) is functionally important for the tumour suppressive effects of LKB1. Our results identify a signalling axis that links YAP activation with LKB1 mutations, and have implications for the treatment of LKB1-mutant human malignancies (cancerous tumors). In addition, our findings provide insight into upstream signals of the Hippo–YAP signalling cascade.

Reverse engineering of TLX oncogenic transcriptional networks identifies RUNX1 as tumor suppressor in T-ALL (T cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia). The TLX1 and TLX3 transcription factor oncogenes play a key role in the pathogenesis of T-ALL (a type of blood cancer that affects immature T cells). Here we used reverse engineering of global transcriptional networks to decipher the oncogenic regulatory circuit controlled by TLX1 and TLX3. This systems biology analysis defined T cell leukemia homeobox 1 (TLX1) and TLX3 as master regulators of an oncogenic transcriptional circuit governing T-ALL. Notably, a network structure analysis of this hierarchical network identified RUNX1 as a key mediator of the T-ALL induced by TLX1 and TLX3 and predicted a tumor-suppressor role for RUNX1 in T cell transformation. Consistent with these results, we identified recurrent somatic loss-of-function mutations in RUNX1 in human T-ALL. Overall, these results place TLX1 and TLX3 at the top of an oncogenic transcriptional network controlling leukemia development, show the power of network analyses to identify key elements in the regulatory circuits governing human cancer and identify RUNX1 as a tumor-suppressor gene (a gene that prevents abnormal cell growth) in T-ALL.

Oncogenic mTOR (mammalian target of rapamycin) signaling recruits myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs, a heterogeneous population of immature myeloid cells with immunosuppressive properties) to promote tumor initiation. MDSCs play critical roles in primary and metastatic cancer progression. MDSC regulation is widely variable even among patients harbouring the same type of malignancy, and the mechanisms governing such heterogeneity are largely unknown. Here, integrating human tumour genomics and syngeneic mammary tumour models, we demonstrate that mTOR signalling in cancer cells dictates a mammary tumour's ability to stimulate MDSC accumulation through regulating G-CSF (granulocyte colony-stimulating factor). Inhibiting this pathway or its activators (for example, FGFR, fibroblast growth factor receptor) impairs tumour progression, which is partially rescued by restoring MDSCs or G-CSF. Tumour-initiating cells (TICs, a subset of cancer cells with self-renewal and differentiation capacity) exhibit elevated G-CSF. MDSCs reciprocally increase TIC frequency through activating Notch in tumour cells, forming a feedforward loop. Analyses of primary breast cancers and patient-derived xenografts corroborate these mechanisms in patients. These findings establish a non-canonical oncogenic role of mTOR signalling in recruiting pro-tumorigenic MDSCs and show how defined cancer subsets may evolve to promote and depend on a distinct immune microenvironment.

LDL-cholesterol concentrations: a genome-wide association study (GWAS) BACKGROUND LDL cholesterol (low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, a key component of atherosclerotic plaques) has a causal role in the development of cardiovascular disease (CVD). Improved understanding of the biological mechanisms that underlie the metabolism and regulation of LDL cholesterol might help to identify novel therapeutic targets. We therefore did a genome-wide association study (GWAS) of LDL-cholesterol concentrations. METHODS We used genome-wide association data from up to 11,685 participants with measures of circulating LDL-cholesterol concentrations across five studies, including data for 293,461 autosomal single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs, DNA sequence variations) with a minor allele frequency of 5% or more that passed our quality control criteria. We also used data from a second genome-wide array in up to 4337 participants from three of these five studies, with data for 290,140 SNPs. We did replication studies in two independent populations consisting of up to 4979 participants. Statistical approaches, including meta-analysis (combining results from multiple studies) and linkage disequilibrium plots (visualizing genetic associations), were used to refine association signals; we analysed pooled data from all seven populations to determine the effect of each SNP on variations in circulating LDL-cholesterol concentrations. FINDINGS In our initial scan, we found two SNPs (rs599839 [p=1.7x10(-15)] and rs4970834 [p=3.0x10(-11)]) that showed genome-wide statistical association with LDL cholesterol at chromosomal locus 1p13.3. The second genome screen found a third statistically associated SNP (rs number, p-value indicating significance).

Characterization of two classes of small molecule inhibitors of Arp2/3 complex (a key regulator of actin filament nucleation involved in various cellular movements) Polymerization of actin filaments directed by the actin-related protein (Arp)2/3 complex (involved in organizing actin filaments) supports many types of cellular movements. However, questions remain regarding the relative contributions of Arp2/3 complex versus other mechanisms of actin filament nucleation to processes such as path finding by neuronal growth cones (neuronal extensions that guide axon growth); this is because of the lack of simple methods to inhibit Arp2/3 complex reversibly in living cells. Here we describe two classes of small molecules that bind to different sites on the Arp2/3 complex and inhibit its ability to nucleate actin filaments. CK-0944636 binds between Arp2 and Arp3, where it appears to block movement of Arp2 and Arp3 into their active conformation (state). CK-0993548 inserts into the hydrophobic core of Arp3 and alters its conformation (shape). Both classes of compounds inhibit formation of actin filament comet tails by Listeria (bacteria that cause food poisoning) and podosomes by monocytes (white blood cells). Two inhibitors with different mechanisms of action provide a powerful approach for studying the Arp2/3 complex in living cells.

Immobilized chemokine fields and soluble chemokine gradients cooperatively shape migration patterns of dendritic cells (DCs). Chemokines orchestrate immune cell trafficking by eliciting either directed or random migration (cells moving towards or away from a stimulus) and by activating integrins (cell-surface receptors) in order to induce cell adhesion (attachment to a surface). Analyzing DC migration, we showed that these distinct cellular responses depended on the mode of chemokine presentation within tissues. The surface-immobilized form of the chemokine CCL21, the heparan sulfate-anchoring ligand of the CC-chemokine receptor 7 (CCR7), caused random movement of DCs that was confined to the chemokine-presenting surface because it triggered integrin-mediated adhesion. Upon direct contact with CCL21, DCs truncated the anchoring residues of CCL21, thereby releasing it from the solid phase. Soluble CCL21 functionally resembles the second CCR7 ligand, CCL19, which lacks anchoring residues and forms soluble gradients. Both soluble CCR7 ligands triggered chemotactic movement (movement towards a chemical stimulus), but not surface adhesion. Adhesive random migration and directional steering cooperate to produce dynamic but spatially restricted locomotion patterns closely resembling the cellular dynamics observed in secondary lymphoid organs (tissues where immune cells gather and interact).

microRNAs (miRNAs): A Safeguard against Turmoil? Emerging data suggest that microRNAs (miRNAs) are instrumental in a variety of stress responses in addition to their more recognized role in development. Surprisingly, miRNAs, which normally suppress expression of target transcripts (by binding to them and preventing translation), may become activators of expression during stress. This might be partially explained by new interactions of miRNA/Argonaute complexes with RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) that relocate from different subcellular compartments (e.g., nucleus to cytoplasm) during stress.

Epigenetic regulation of miR-184 by Methyl-CpG binding protein 1 (MBD1) governs neural stem cell (aNSC) proliferation and differentiation. Methyl-CpG binding protein 1 (MBD1) regulates gene expression via a DNA methylation-mediated epigenetic mechanism (processes involving chemical modifications to DNA that alter gene activity without changing the DNA sequence). We have previously demonstrated that Methyl-CpG binding protein 1 (MBD1) deficiency impairs adult neural stem/progenitor cell (aNSC) differentiation and neurogenesis (generation of new neurons from neural stem cells), but the underlying mechanism was unclear. Here, we show that Methyl-CpG binding protein 1 (MBD1) regulates the expression of several microRNAs (small non-coding RNA molecules that can regulate gene expression) in aNSCs and, specifically, that miR-184 is directly repressed by Methyl-CpG binding protein 1 (MBD1). High levels of microRNA 184 (miR-184) promoted proliferation but inhibited differentiation of aNSCs, whereas inhibition of microRNA 184 (miR-184) rescued the phenotypes associated with Methyl-CpG binding protein 1 (MBD1) deficiency. We further found that microRNA 184 (miR-184) regulates the expression of Numblike (Numbl), a known regulator of brain development (processes that guide the formation and organization of the nervous system), by binding to the 3'-untranslated region (3'-UTR) of Numbl mRNA and affecting its translation (the process of synthesizing proteins from the information encoded in messenger RNA). Expression of exogenous Numblike (Numbl) could rescue the aNSC defects that result from either microRNA 184 (miR-184) overexpression or Methyl-CpG binding protein 1 (MBD1) deficiency. Therefore, Methyl-CpG binding protein 1 (MBD1), microRNA 184 (miR-184), and Numblike (Numbl) form a regulatory network that helps control the balance between proliferation and differentiation of aNSCs.

Association between maternal serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25-OHD, a form of vitamin D that is measured in blood) level and pregnancy and neonatal outcomes: systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies. OBJECTIVE To assess the effect of 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25-OHD) levels on pregnancy outcomes and birth variables. DESIGN Systematic review and meta-analysis. DATA SOURCES Medline (1966 to August 2012), PubMed (2008 to August 2012), Embase (1980 to August 2012), CINAHL (1981 to August 2012), the Cochrane database of systematic reviews, and the Cochrane database of registered clinical trials. STUDY SELECTION Studies reporting on the association between serum 25-OHD levels during pregnancy and the outcomes of interest (pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, bacterial vaginosis, caesarean section, small for gestational age infants, birth weight, birth length, and head circumference). DATA EXTRACTION Two authors independently extracted data from original research articles, including key indicators of study quality. We pooled the most adjusted odds ratios and weighted mean differences. Associations were tested in subgroups representing different patient characteristics and study quality. RESULTS 3357 studies were identified and reviewed for eligibility. 31 eligible studies were included in the final analysis. Insufficient serum levels of 25-OHD were associated with gestational diabetes (pooled odds ratio 1.49, 95% confidence interval 1.18 to 1.89), pre-eclampsia (1.79, 1.25 to 2.58), and small for gestational age infants (1.85, 95% confidence interval 1.23 to 2.78).
To clarify, 25-OHD (a form of vitamin D that is measured in blood) was linked to an increased risk of gestational diabetes, pre-eclampsia, and small for gestational age infants when levels were insufficient. The confidence intervals indicate the range within which the true effect size is likely to lie with a certain level of confidence (e.g., 95%). In this case, the confidence intervals do not include 1, suggesting a statistically significant association. The pooled odds ratios represent the combined estimate of the odds of an outcome occurring given a specific exposure (low 25-OHD levels) compared to the general population. The results suggest that maintaining adequate 25-OHD levels during pregnancy may be beneficial for reducing the risk of these adverse outcomes. Further research is needed to confirm these findings and explore potential mechanisms. Hyaluronan (HA, a large-molecule glycosaminoglycan) impairs vascular function and drug delivery in a mouse model of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDA, a type of cancer arising from the epithelial lining of the pancreas). OBJECTIVE Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDA) is characterised by stromal desmoplasia (excessive fibrous tissue formation) and vascular dysfunction (impaired blood vessel function), which critically impair drug delivery. This study examines the role of an abundant extracellular matrix component, the megadalton glycosaminoglycan hyaluronan (HA), as a novel therapeutic target in PDA. METHODS Using a genetically engineered mouse model of PDA, the authors enzymatically depleted HA by a clinically formulated PEGylated human recombinant PH20 hyaluronidase (PEGPH20) and examined tumour perfusion, vascular permeability and drug delivery. The preclinical utility of PEGPH20 in combination with gemcitabine (a chemotherapy agent) was assessed by short-term and survival studies. RESULTS PEGPH20 rapidly and sustainably depleted HA, inducing the re-expansion of PDA blood vessels and increasing the intratumoral delivery of two chemotherapeutic agents, doxorubicin (an anthracycline antibiotic used in cancer treatment) and gemcitabine. Moreover, PEGPH20 triggered fenestrations (small openings in the endothelium) and interendothelial junctional gaps in PDA tumour endothelia and promoted a tumour-specific increase in macromolecular permeability. Finally, combination therapy with PEGPH20 and gemcitabine led to inhibition of PDA tumour growth and prolonged survival over gemcitabine monotherapy, suggesting immediate clinical utility. CONCLUSIONS The authors demonstrate that hyaluronan (HA) impedes the intratumoral vasculature in PDA and propose that its enzymatic depletion be explored as a means to improve drug delivery and treatment outcomes.
To further explore the mechanisms underlying the effects of PEGPH20 on PDA, the authors also investigated the impact of HA depletion on the expression of key angiogenic factors and the activation status of relevant signalling pathways. These findings provide valuable insights into the potential therapeutic benefits of targeting HA in PDA and pave the way for future clinical trials. Lysine acetylation regulates various metabolic processes, such as fatty acid oxidation (the process of breaking down fatty acids to release energy), Krebs cycle (also known as citric acid cycle, a central metabolic pathway that generates ATP), oxidative phosphorylation (the process of generating ATP through the electron transport chain), angiogenesis (the formation of new blood vessels), and so on. Thus disorders of lysine acetylation may be correlated with obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, which are termed as the metabolic complications. With accumulating studies on proteomic acetylation, lysine acetylation also involves in cell immune status and degenerative diseases, for example, Alzheimer’s disease (a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by memory loss and cognitive decline) and Huntington’s disease (a genetic disorder that causes parts of the brain to deteriorate over time, leading to uncontrolled movements, emotional problems, and loss of thinking ability). This review primarily summarizes the current studies of lysine acetylation in metabolism modulation and in metabolism-related diseases, such as cardiovascular disease and fat metabolism disorder.

Coupled local translation and degradation regulate growth cone collapse (a specialized region at the tip of an axon involved in neuronal wiring). Local translation mediates axonal responses to Semaphorin3A (Sema3A, a guidance molecule that influences axonal pathfinding) and other guidance cues. However, only a subset of the axonal proteome is locally synthesized, whereas most proteins are trafficked from the soma (cell body). The reason why only specific proteins are locally synthesized is unknown. Here we show that local protein synthesis and degradation are linked events in growth cones. We find that growth cones exhibit high levels of ubiquitination (the process by which ubiquitin molecules are attached to target proteins, marking them for degradation), and that local signalling pathways trigger the ubiquitination and degradation of RhoA, a mediator of Sema3A-induced growth cone collapse. Inhibition of RhoA degradation is sufficient to remove the protein-synthesis requirement for Sema3A-induced growth cone collapse. In addition to RhoA, we find that locally translated proteins are the main targets of the ubiquitin-proteasome system in growth cones. Thus, local protein degradation is a major feature of growth cones and creates a requirement for local translation to replenish proteins needed to maintain growth cone responses.

Alcohol Intake and Blood Pressure: A Systematic Review Implementing a Mendelian Randomization Approach (Mendelian randomization is a method used to investigate causal relationships between genetic variants and health outcomes by leveraging genetic markers that are randomly assigned at conception and are not influenced by environmental or lifestyle factors). BACKGROUND Alcohol has been reported to be a common and modifiable risk factor for hypertension (high blood pressure). However, observational studies are subject to confounding by other behavioural and sociodemographic factors, while clinical trials are difficult to implement and have limited follow-up time. Mendelian randomization can provide robust evidence on the nature of this association by use of a common polymorphism in aldehyde dehydrogenase 2 (ALDH2, an enzyme involved in alcohol metabolism) as a surrogate for measuring alcohol consumption. ALDH2 encodes a major enzyme involved in alcohol metabolism. Individuals homozygous for the null variant (*2*2) experience adverse symptoms when drinking alcohol and consequently drink considerably less alcohol than wild-type homozygotes (*1*1) or heterozygotes. We hypothesise that this polymorphism may influence the risk of hypertension by affecting alcohol drinking behaviour. METHODS AND FINDINGS We carried out fixed effect meta-analyses of the ALDH2 genotype with blood pressure (five studies, n = 7,658) and hypertension (three studies, n = 4,219) using studies identified via systematic review. In males, we obtained an overall odds ratio of 2.42 (95% confidence interval [CI] 1.66-3.55, p = 4.8 x 10(-6)) for hypertension comparing *1*1 with *2*2 homozygotes and an odds ratio of 1.72 (95% CI 1.17-2.52, p = 0.006) comparing heterozygotes (surrogate for moderate drinkers) with *2*2 homozygotes.

Unique response pathways are established by allosteric interactions among nuclear hormone receptors (proteins that bind hormones and regulate gene expression). Heterodimerization is a common paradigm among eukaryotic transcription factors (proteins that help turn genes on or off). The 9-cis retinoic acid receptor (RXR; a nuclear receptor that binds 9-cis retinoic acid and regulates gene expression) serves as a common heterodimerization partner for several nuclear receptors, including the thyroid hormone receptor (T3R; a nuclear receptor that binds thyroid hormones and regulates gene expression) and retinoic acid receptor (RAR; a nuclear receptor that binds retinoic acid and regulates gene expression). This raises the question as to whether these complexes possess dual hormonal responsiveness. We devised a strategy to examine the transcriptional properties of each receptor individually or when tethered to a heterodimeric partner. We find that the intrinsic binding properties of RXR are masked in T3R-RXR and RAR-RXR heterodimers. In contrast, RXR is active as a non-DNA-binding cofactor with the NGFI-B/Nurr1 orphan receptors (nuclear receptors without known endogenous ligands). Heterodimerization of RXR with constitutively active NGFI-B/Nurr1 creates a novel hormone-dependent complex. These findings suggest that allosteric interactions among heterodimers create complexes with unique properties. We suggest that allostery is a critical feature underlying the generation of diversity in hormone response networks.

3315558: Genetic analysis of human obesity in an Italian sample (study focusing on genetic factors contributing to obesity in a specific population). An analysis of the genetic factors in obesity has been carried out on a sample of nuclear families from Aosta (N. Italy) (a region in northern Italy). The families consisted of the parents and siblings (sibs) of all elementary school children considered to be obese during a preliminary screening (identification of children with obesity) and a similar sample of non-obese children and their nuclear families (families consisting of parents and siblings of children without obesity). The numbers of such families were 67 and 112, respectively (67 families with obese children and 112 families with non-obese children).

Human TRPML1 channel structures in open and closed conformations Transient receptor potential mucolipin 1 (TRPML1 (cation channel that allows passage of Ca2+ ions and other cations through the cell membrane, involved in calcium signaling and homeostasis of lysosomes)) is a Ca2+-releasing cation channel that mediates the calcium signalling and homeostasis of lysosomes. Mutations in TRPML1 lead to mucolipidosis type IV (genetic disorder characterized by lysosomal storage of lipids and impaired lysosomal function), a severe lysosomal storage disorder. Here we report two electron cryo-microscopy structures of full-length human TRPML1: a 3.72-Å apo structure at pH 7.0 in the closed state, and a 3.49-Å agonist-bound structure at pH 6.0 in an open state. Several aromatic and hydrophobic residues in pore helix 1, helices S5 and S6, and helix S6 of a neighbouring subunit, form a hydrophobic cavity to house the agonist, suggesting a distinct agonist-binding site from that found in TRPV1 (transient receptor potential vanilloid 1, a TRP channel from a different subfamily). The opening of TRPML1 is associated with distinct dilations of its lower gate together with a slight structural movement of pore helix 1. Our work reveals the regulatory mechanism of TRPML channels, facilitates better understanding of TRP channel activation, and provides insights into the molecular basis of mucolipidosis type IV pathogenesis.

Inflammatory signaling in human Tuberculosis granulomas is spatially organized (the distribution of inflammatory molecules is not uniform throughout the granuloma). Granulomas are the pathological hallmark of tuberculosis (TB; a chronic bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis), but their function and mechanisms of formation remain poorly understood. To understand the role of granulomas in TB, we analyzed the proteomes of granulomas from subjects with tuberculosis in an unbiased manner. Using laser-capture microdissection (a technique for isolating specific cell types or regions from a tissue section), mass spectrometry (a method for identifying and quantifying proteins based on their mass-to-charge ratio), and confocal microscopy (a microscopy technique providing high-resolution images of thin optical sections of thick specimens), we generated detailed molecular maps of human granulomas. We found that the centers of granulomas have a pro-inflammatory environment (characterized by the presence of antimicrobial peptides, reactive oxygen species (ROS; molecules involved in cellular defense against pathogens), and pro-inflammatory eicosanoids (lipid mediators of inflammation)). Conversely, the tissue surrounding the caseum (caseous necrotic material at the center of the granuloma) has a comparatively anti-inflammatory signature. These findings are consistent across a set of six human subjects and in rabbits. Although the balance between systemic pro- and anti-inflammatory signals is crucial to TB disease outcome, here we find that these signals are physically segregated within each granuloma. From the protein and lipid snapshots of human and rabbit lesions analyzed here, we hypothesize that the pathologic response to TB is shaped by the precise anatomical localization of these inflammatory pathways during the development of the granuloma.

Mitochondrial iron chelation ameliorates cigarette-smoke (CS) induced bronchitis and emphysema in mice. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is linked to both cigarette smoking and genetic determinants. We have previously identified iron-responsive element-binding protein 2 (IRP2, a protein that regulates iron metabolism) as an important COPD susceptibility gene and have shown that IRP2 protein is increased in the lungs of individuals with COPD. Here we demonstrate that mice deficient in Irp2 (gene encoding IRP2) were protected from cigarette smoke (CS)-induced experimental COPD. By integrating RNA immunoprecipitation followed by sequencing (RIP-seq, a technique for identifying RNA binding proteins and their target mRNAs) and RNA sequencing (RNA-seq, a method for measuring transcript abundance), and gene expression and functional enrichment clustering analysis, we identified Irp2 as a regulator of mitochondrial function in the lungs of mice. IRP2 increased mitochondrial iron loading and levels of cytochrome c oxidase (COX, a complex involved in the electron transport chain) which led to mitochondrial dysfunction and subsequent experimental COPD. Frataxin-deficient mice, which had higher mitochondrial iron loading, showed impaired airway mucociliary clearance (MCC, the process by which mucus and particles are removed from the respiratory tract) and higher pulmonary inflammation at baseline, whereas mice deficient in the synthesis of cytochrome c oxidase, which have reduced COX, were protected from CS-induced pulmonary inflammation and impairment of MCC. Mice treated with a mitochondrial iron chelator (a molecule that binds to and removes iron from mitochondria) or mice fed a low-iron diet were protected from CS-induced COPD. Mesenchymal Inflammation Drives Genotoxic Stress in Hematopoietic Stem Cells and Predicts Disease Evolution in Human Pre-leukemia. Mesenchymal niche cells may drive tissue failure and malignant transformation in the hematopoietic system (blood-forming tissues), but the underlying molecular mechanisms and relevance to human disease remain poorly defined. Here, we show that perturbation of mesenchymal cells in a mouse model of the pre-leukemic disorder Shwachman-Diamond syndrome (SDS) (a rare genetic disorder characterized by bone marrow failure and pancreatic insufficiency) induces mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and activation of DNA damage responses in hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (cells that give rise to all blood cells). Massive parallel RNA sequencing of highly purified mesenchymal cells in the SDS mouse model and a range of human pre-leukemic syndromes identified p53-S100A8/9-TLR inflammatory signaling as a common driving mechanism of genotoxic stress (damage to DNA caused by chemical, physical, or biological agents). Transcriptional activation of this signaling axis in the mesenchymal niche predicted leukemic evolution and progression-free survival in myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) (a group of disorders characterized by low blood-cell counts and ineffective bone marrow production), the principal leukemia predisposition syndrome. Collectively, our findings identify mesenchymal niche-induced genotoxic stress in heterotypic stem and progenitor cells through inflammatory signaling as a targetable determinant of disease outcome in human pre-leukemia.

Broad antiretroviral defence by human APOBEC3G through lethal editing of nascent reverse transcripts (process by which retroviruses synthesize DNA from their RNA genome). Viral replication usually requires that innate intracellular lines of defence be overcome, a task usually accomplished by specialized viral gene products. The virion infectivity factor (Vif) protein of human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) is required during the late stages of viral production to counter the antiviral activity of APOBEC3G (apolipoprotein B mRNA-editing enzyme, catalytic polypeptide-like 3G; also known as CEM15), a protein expressed notably in human T lymphocytes. When produced in the presence of APOBEC3G, vif-defective virus is non-infectious. APOBEC3G is closely related to APOBEC1, the central component of an RNA-editing complex that deaminates a cytosine residue in apoB messenger RNA (apoB mRNA, a transcript of the apolipoprotein B gene). APOBEC family members also have potent DNA mutator activity through dC deamination; however, whether the editing potential of APOBEC3G has any relevance to HIV inhibition is unknown. Here, we demonstrate that it does, as APOBEC3G exerts its antiviral effect during reverse transcription (process by which retroviruses synthesize DNA from their RNA genome) to trigger G-to-A hypermutation in the nascent retroviral DNA. We also find that APOBEC3G can act on a broad range of retroviruses in addition to HIV, suggesting that hypermutation by editing is a general innate defence mechanism against this important group of pathogens.
